episodes.title: Emerging Markets Outlook and Strategy for 2H26
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
JPMorgan's 2H26 mid-year EM outlook designates EM FX as the most preferred asset class, local rates neutral with selective carry, and sovereign credit near 20-year tights. Global growth is tracking 2.5%, EM at 4% (ex-China 3.5%), underpinned by the AI/tech capex cycle, broadening labor markets, and still-supportive financial conditions. The expected US-Iran deal and Strait of Hormuz reopening this week are the near-term bullish catalyst, with oil settling around $80 the base case.

**Key points**
- EM YTD returns: local FX +6.3%, sovereign credit +1.5–3%, EM equities +22.2%
- Core inflation running ~50bp above EM central bank targets; headline likely peaked May/June if oil holds near $80, but goods and services pass-through still working through in H2
- EM central bank split: 6 hiking, 5 cutting; defensive hikers (BI/IDR, BSP/PHP, Turkey, South Africa) respond to FX/credibility pressure — pressure may ease with lower oil; standard tighteners (BoK/KRW, RBI/INR) driven by domestic macro; high yielders (BCB/BRL, Hungary) cutting
- Fed on hold is the EM anchor; real yield repricing is flagged as the more damaging tail risk vs nominal moves
- EMBIG at ~230bp, MBX CCC ~17bp from 2007 all-time tight; all-in yield ~6.25%; growth narrative is the only credible widening catalyst
- Colombia second round imminent (right-center candidate leading), Peru vote count nearing conclusion, Brazil presidential election in fall (Lula competitive in early polling)

**FX / rates implications**
- EM FX long preferred in mid-to-high yielders with proactive CB tightening and no BOP stress — EMEA and LATAM broadly favored over EM Asia where energy-import drag is concentrated
- MXN underperforms LATAM peers on fiscal constraints and tighter domestic financial conditions
- IDR and PHP defensive-hike premium may compress if Hormuz deal and lower oil reduce BOP pressure; watch BI and BSP forward guidance
- Local rates neutral overall; high-yielder carry (BRL, select LATAM) most likely to flip bullish on lower oil anchoring inflation expectations; KRW and INR curves pricing further hikes
- EM credit: stay long near tights — only a growth shock dislodges spreads from here

**Watch list**
- US-Iran deal signing and Strait of Hormuz reopening (this week — binary catalyst)
- Brent trajectory — $80 as the threshold for H2 CPI path and CB reaction functions
- Fed tone — any tightening pivot is the primary systemic EM tail risk; watch real yield channel
- Colombia second-round presidential election (~1 week out; right-center favored)
- Peru final count
- Brazil presidential election (fall 2026; market scrutiny intensifies as Lula race tightens)
- US midterms (fall 2026; trade and fiscal policy implications for EM, particularly South America geopolitics)

episodes.title: You've Never Had It So Good: The Reality of Real-Time Revealed
episodes.feed: TMI TreasuryCast (HSBC Treasury Beyond Borders inside)
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
This TMI TreasuryCast episode covers corporate treasury technology adoption — specifically real-time payments, Swift Instant Cash Reporting (ICR), ISO 20022 standardisation, and AI-driven cash flow forecasting. Speakers are from Equinor (head of cash management infrastructure) and Deutsche Bank (global head of cash management). The discussion is operational and vendor-facing, with no market or macro content.

**Key points**
- Equinor has centralised treasury onto a single ERP with an in-house bank and cash pooling structure, now layering real-time visibility via Swift ICR through a single Deutsche Bank API integration.
- Real-time data is framed as intraday operational tool — payment confirmations, anomaly detection, intraday credit-line release — not a driver of long-term liquidity positioning.
- Deutsche Bank urges bank-agnostic standardisation so multi-bank corporates can aggregate data without bespoke connections per institution.
- AI use cases highlighted: cash flow forecasting and order-to-cash reconciliation; Equinor is piloting AI forecasting but leadership acknowledges 100% accuracy is unattainable.
- Governance emphasis: clear mandate rules are required to prevent real-time data generating noise or micro-management.

**Watch list**
- Broader ISO 20022 migration timeline and Swift ICR adoption curve — relevant as a plumbing layer for EM payment corridors (LATAM, APAC) where Equinor operates, though no specific market catalysts flagged in this episode.

episodes.title: Carmen Li's Plan to Build a Futures Market for Compute
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Carmen Lee, CEO of Compute Exchange and Silicon Data, outlines the build-out of GPU compute futures and options in partnership with CME Group (pending CME approval), positioning GPU capacity as a tradeable commodity analogous to oil. The episode is primarily a market-structure story about a nascent asset class; direct transmission to EM FX or rates is minimal, but the AI capex super-cycle and its semiconductor supply-chain implications remain a background factor for TWD and KRW exposures.

**Key points**
- Silicon Data launched the world's first GPU price indices on the Bloomberg terminal in 2025, drawing on 100+ data sources and 150,000+ daily traded prices; CME futures/options launch expected within months, pending CME approval.
- H100 on-demand GPU rental rates rose ~8% over the past three months; B200 prices, after an initial post-launch dip, have rebounded above launch levels — a steeper demand recovery than prior chip-generation cycles (A100, H100), suggesting a tighter supply/demand equilibrium than consensus expected.
- Measured daily volatility on the H100 index runs 20-30%, in line with liquid commodity benchmarks, supporting the hedging rationale.
- Contracts will be financially settled (not physical delivery), mirroring oil futures mechanics; basis risk between index price and actual cloud-provider cost is acknowledged and modelled.
- GPU residual value study: H100 chips retain ~85 cents on the dollar one year post-use, ~84 cents at year two — better than auto depreciation curves, relevant for balance-sheet holders financing GPU inventory.
- 38% documented performance variance for nominally identical A100 chips across providers is the core fungibility problem that indices must normalise; Silicon Data uses six months of historical trades and factor decomposition to address this.

**Watch list**
- CME regulatory approval for GPU futures/options — the key catalyst for market launch.
- B200 and H100 on-demand price trends as new data-centre capacity (announced 25bn+ investments) translates into live GPU availability.

episodes.title: The Bull Case After the Pullback in Stocks
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Morgan Stanley CIO Mike Wilson argues the recent S&P 500 pullback is a healthy correction within an intact earnings-driven bull market, caused by a peak in the rate of change of earnings revisions (led by semiconductors) and a deceleration in systemwide liquidity — not a cycle turn. For EM FX/rates traders, the more actionable takeaway is Wilson's view that the dollar, crude oil, and US interest rates may all be simultaneously peaking, which would ease the three key headwinds for EM.

**Key points**
- S&P 500 year-end target held at 8,000; earnings revision breadth above 70% signals rate-of-change deceleration, not outright downturn — next-12-month estimates still rising
- Fed's Reserve Management Program has slowed from ~$40bn/month in April to ~$10bn now; Treasury buybacks also tapering — the combined ~$500bn liquidity boost of early 2025 is unwinding at the margin
- Kevin Worsh chairs his first FOMC meeting this week; Wilson watches the bond market reaction more than the rate decision itself; UST 10y at 4.5% and calm funding markets are the key thresholds
- Strait of Hormuz reopening deal (Iran) announced over the weekend; could ease crude prices and remove a stagflation tail risk
- Leadership rotation underway toward consumer discretionary, transports, and regional banks — all up >10% past month vs. S&P 500 modestly lower

**FX / rates implications**
- A peaking DXY is broadly positive for EM FX; carry trades in INR, IDR, BRL, and MXN would benefit from easing USD funding pressure and improved real-yield differentials
- UST 10y holding below 4.5% is pivotal for EM bond flows; a sustained break above that level would trigger risk-off and pressure EM local rates and sovereign spreads
- Oil easing via the Hormuz deal benefits oil-importing EMs — positive for INR, PHP, and IDR via improved current account dynamics and lower inflation pass-through
- Liquidity deceleration (Reserve Management Program tapering) continues to unwind crowded momentum trades globally; EM carry positions with high beta to US liquidity conditions remain exposed to further deleveraging

**Watch list**
- FOMC this week: Worsh's tone and bond market reaction; UST 10y 4.5% as the line in the sand for EM flows
- Hormuz/Iran deal durability — monitor Brent and WTI for confirmation of oil price relief
- Earnings revision breadth rolling from 70%+ extremes — semiconductors as leading indicator for broader risk-off risk

episodes.title: Iran Peace Framework, Tech Takes Off, and Investing in World Cup Infrastructure 6/15/26
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
A US-Iran 60-day ceasefire MOU — with Strait of Hormuz reopening imminent — sent WTI down 5% to briefly sub-$80/bbl, its lowest since March, erasing the war risk premium and sparking a broad risk-on rally: S&P +2%, Nasdaq +3%, Dow at record highs. For EM, this is a bifurcated shock — terms-of-trade tailwind for oil-importing Asia (INR, PHP, IDR, KRW, THB) offset against headwinds for oil-linked LATAM (MXN, COP, BRL), with the risk-on impulse providing a cross-cutting lift to carry and flows.

**Key points**
- US-Iran MOU signed digitally Sunday; 60-day ceasefire with intensive nuclear talks and sanctions discussions to follow; Strait of Hormuz reopening confirmed toll-free for the duration; full MOU text to be released within 48hrs of Friday signing ceremony
- Sanctions relief explicitly NOT in the 60-day deal; post-60-day regime — including possible tolls — remains unresolved, creating residual geopolitical risk premium that analysts (Janus Henderson's Rich Bernstein) say will persist
- Strait normalisation will take 3-6 months per Sparta: upstream restart months 1-2, refineries month 2, petrochemicals month 3; current transit only 2.9mn bpd vs 15mn bpd pre-war per Kepler
- WTI briefly below $80 (March low) but still well above pre-war ~$65; major operators (Maris) not changing regional operations yet, watching flow confirmation
- US LNG/crude exports up ~3mn bpd YoY; Argentina flagged as a major growth supply source, reducing long-term Hormuz dependency
- CPI print due Wednesday; Bernstein argues inflation is structural, not purely war-driven — pro-inflation trade intact regardless of Iran outcome
- SpaceX IPO day 2 (+13% after +19% Friday) amplifying broader risk-on; AI/chip complex ripping (AMD +7%, Western Digital +15%, Nvidia +3% at $212)
- G7 Geneva summit underway; leaders backing toll-free Strait reopening and diversification away from Hormuz dependency

**FX / rates implications**
- Sub-$80 WTI is a structural terms-of-trade tailwind for large oil-importing EM: INR, PHP, IDR, KRW, THB should see current-account relief and reduced inflationary pressure, supporting central banks with less urgency to defend currencies
- Oil-linked LATAM names (MXN, COP, BRL) face crude headwind but risk-on and carry dynamics are a partial offset; COP most exposed given Colombia's fiscal dependence on oil revenues
- Broad risk-on (equities at records, VIX compression implied) compresses EM risk premia and supports carry trades; watch DXY for follow-through softening which would amplify EM FX gains
- Residual uncertainty over post-60-day Strait regime and slow supply normalisation (3-6 months) caps the disinflationary impulse — limits how aggressively EM central banks can pivot dovish
- Sticky US inflation (Bernstein view confirmed by Wednesday CPI) keeps Fed cuts pinched, capping EM fixed-income rally even as oil falls

**Watch list**
- WTI/Brent price action around $80/$84 — sustained break lower confirms risk-premium removal; failure to hold opens re-pricing
- Wednesday US CPI print — key for Fed cut path and EM rates direction; oil drop should show in headline but watch core
- Kepler/Sparta Strait of Hormuz flow data weekly — tanker traffic normalisation is the real supply signal
- Full MOU text release (24-48hrs post-Friday) — sanctions and toll language details for energy market guidance
- 60-day ceasefire deadline — nuclear negotiation progress; re-closure risk if talks stall
- Argentina upstream energy ramp — medium-term alternative supply source reducing Hormuz dependency premium

episodes.title: How Large Is Private Credit’s Total Addressable Market, Really?
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
PIMCO's Lotfi Karui argues that private credit's true total addressable market is $6–8 trillion — an order of magnitude below commonly cited figures — once assets already securitised, held by insurers, or in dedicated ABF vehicles are stripped out. The analysis is relevant to macro traders primarily through the capital-allocation channel: the pace at which bank balance sheets migrate into private hands shapes institutional risk appetite and the pool of capital competing for EM assets.

**Key points**
- US loans and contractual cash flows total ~$25 trillion; ~$13 trillion already securitised, insurers hold $1.5–2 trillion, dedicated ABF portfolios account for ~$800 billion.
- Residual migration opportunity is ~$9–10 trillion gross, but banks will retain high-quality loans post-regulatory relief, and some assets are operationally hard to transfer, shrinking realistic TAM to $6–8 trillion.
- Direct lending TAM is routinely overstated by conflating BSL/HY outstanding balances with substitutable flow — PIMCO views this as a categorical error.
- ABF sub-categories: residential mortgages (~$3 trillion on bank/non-depository sheets), CRE (~$2.3 trillion), consumer credit (~$4 trillion unsecuritised), equipment finance (~$1.1 trillion), and esoteric cash flows (~$500 billion including aircraft leases).
- GSE-backed conforming mortgages (~$10.1 trillion) explicitly excluded as non-contestable by private capital.

**Watch list**
- Regulatory relief trajectory for US banks — determines how much of the $9–10 trillion gross pool actually migrates to asset managers and competes with EM allocations for institutional capital.

episodes.title: Anatomy of a Recession Update: Resilience
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Clearbridge's Jeff Scholsey calls the US mid-cycle with 11/12 recession indicators green following a NIPA profit-margin upgrade. May NFP (172k, 3-month avg jumps to 188k) printed hawkish, shifting Fed funds pricing more than 100bp since February — from three cuts to 1.5 hikes by mid-2027 — with UST 10y back at 4.5%. For EM traders the actionable reads are oil timing, a structural USD weakening thesis, and commodity-linked EM upside.

**Key points**
- May NFP 172k; 3-month avg 188k; labor ex-healthcare +377k YTD vs -271k all of 2025 — broadening breadth, confirming hawkish Fed repricing
- Oil: Strait of Hormuz effectively closed three months; inventories ~200mb above early-2025 but converging fast — 2025 lows hit ~early June, 2022 trough (prior energy-shock minimum) ~early August; Scholsey flags July as when prices begin moving "materially higher"
- Section 301 tariffs: 10–12.5% on 60 economies covering 99% of 2025 US imports; elevated rates on China and Vietnam maintained; effective tariff rate at 7%, could rise to 11%; tariff refunds expected to offset net drag
- MSCI EM 2026 EPS revised +28% YTD vs +7% for MSCI USA; hyperscaler capex $700B (2026), $875B (2027) underpins TWD and KRW chip-earnings visibility through 2028
- Structural USD bear thesis: admin preference for weak dollar, central-bank reserve diversification, debt-sustainability concerns; 0.6 correlation between rolling 5-year DXY trend and US vs ACWI ex-US outperformance

**FX / rates implications**
- Fed hawkish repricing (UST 10y at 4.5%, 1.5 hikes priced by mid-2027) is a broad EM carry headwind; further UST selloff likely if oil breaks higher in July–August
- Oil July–August inflection = Brent spike = wider current-account deficits for net energy importers INR, IDR, PHP, MXN, COP; USD/EM broadly supported
- Middle East resolution = oil lower + USD weaker = binary EM risk-on; Scholsey's base case is structural USD weakness over 5–10 years, supporting EM FX medium-term
- Metals supercycle directly positive for CLP (copper), BRL (iron ore, agri), IDR (nickel); Chinese consumer recovery adds further upside to this cluster
- Section 301 elevated rates on Vietnam maintain VND pressure and complicate supply-chain rerouting from China

**Watch list**
- Oil inventories hitting early-2025 levels (~early June) and 2022 trough (~early August) — key price-inflection triggers for EM energy-importer currencies
- Middle East ceasefire/resolution — binary risk-on, USD-negative catalyst; watch timing vs inventory drawdown
- UST 10y 4.5% and next FOMC dot plot for confirmation of hawkish repricing
- Section 301 rollout after 3–4 week pause; Vietnam and China supply-chain exposure
